title: Byte-level tokenization
summary: Tokenization that operates on raw bytes so any Unicode string maps to a small, fixed base alphabet.
openingSummary: Byte-level tokenization feeds bytes or byte-derived symbols into subword merges, which prevents out-of-vocabulary Unicode surprises at the cost of longer sequences for some languages.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: Byte-level schemes map text to a compact byte alphabet—often 256 symbols—before applying BPE-style merges. GPT-2 popularized byte-level BPE for English and multilingual robustness. Any valid UTF-8 string tokenizes without unknown-token failures at the character level.

whyItMatters.title: Why It Matters
whyItMatters.body: Byte-level tokenization reduces brittle preprocessing and helps models handle typos, rare emoji, and mixed scripts. It can increase token count versus word-piece vocabularies tuned to a single language, which affects context windows and billing.

simpleExample.title: Simple Example
simpleExample.body: An uncommon Unicode character might become a short byte sequence merged into one or two tokens instead of triggering an unknown-token fallback.

commonConfusions.title: Common Confusions
commonConfusions.body: Byte-level tokenization is not the same as character-level models that attend over graphemes without subword merges. It is also not identical to SentencePiece—SPM can be byte-aware but the terms are not interchangeable.
